target.title: Fix Feishu card action mention gating
target.body:
Fixes #85450.

## Summary

- Mark Feishu synthetic group card-action callbacks with bot mention metadata when `botOpenId` is known.
- Keep p2p card callbacks unchanged so direct-message callbacks do not gain synthetic mentions.
- Add regression coverage for both the group mention metadata and the p2p no-mention behavior.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Feishu structured card button callbacks in `requireMention: true` group chats were synthesized as group text messages without bot mention metadata, so the existing group activation gate could treat the callback as not addressing the bot and drop it. After this patch, synthetic group card callbacks include a bot mention entry when `botOpenId` is available, while p2p callbacks still omit synthetic mentions.

Real environment tested: Local macOS OpenClaw checkout at `/Users/googlerest/.openclaw/workspace/openclaw-src`, branch `codex/fix-85450-feishu-card-mention`, commit `2482f7f442 Fix Feishu card action mention gating`.

```bash
git diff --check
```

target.title: [codex] Suppress heartbeat inbound metadata
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes #97067.

Cron-driven heartbeat turns could inherit the resolved delivery route in `OriginatingTo` and then build a user-role `Conversation info (untrusted metadata)` block from that synthetic route. When the stored route was an unqualified channel target such as `c2c:...`, the heartbeat prompt looked like partial inbound chat metadata instead of a plain scheduled heartbeat turn.

## Why This Change Was Made

Heartbeat runs are synthetic system turns, not real inbound channel messages. The heartbeat prompt already carries the required model-facing instruction, and routing/delivery policy still uses the session context separately. Suppressing the inbound user-context block for heartbeat runs avoids presenting last-channel delivery metadata as untrusted per-message metadata while leaving normal inbound metadata behavior unchanged.

## User Impact

Agents should no longer see heartbeat polls wrapped in partial `Conversation info` metadata, reducing false prompt-injection warnings and avoiding noisy cross-channel alert broadcasts caused by those warnings.

## Evidence

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply-run.media-only.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/inbound-meta.test.ts src/infra/heartbeat-runner.returns-default-unset.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local`

Remote changed-gate attempts were blocked by local auth/tooling, so PR CI should be treated as the broad gate:

- Testbox-through-Crabbox: blocked because local Blacksmith is not authenticated to org `openclaw`.
- Azure Crabbox: blocked because `az`/Azure auth is not available locally.
- AWS Crabbox: blocked because AWS credentials are not available locally.

target.title: fix(qwen): enable qwen3.6-plus on Coding Plan CN, correct reasoning flag
target.body:
## Summary

- **What changed**: Enables `qwen3.6-plus` on the DashScope Coding Plan CN endpoint (`coding.dashscope.aliyuncs.com`) and corrects its `reasoning` flag (`false` → `true`).
  - `extensions/qwen/models.ts`: `reasoning: false` → `reasoning: true` on the `qwen3.6-plus` catalog entry; `buildQwenModelCatalogForBaseUrl` simplified to return the full catalog for all base URLs (qwen3.6-plus no longer excluded from Coding Plan URLs).
  - `extensions/qwen/openclaw.plugin.json`: removes the two `modelCatalog.suppressions` entries that blocked `qwen3.6-plus` from Coding Plan configurations — confirmed available on `coding.dashscope.aliyuncs.com`.
  - `extensions/qwen/index.ts`: removes `normalizeConfig` (which stripped `qwen3.6-plus` from Coding Plan `providerConfig.models`); no longer needed after manifest suppression removal.
- **Why it matters**: `qwen3.6-plus` is available on `coding.dashscope.aliyuncs.com` (DashScope CN Coding Plan). The existing suppression incorrectly hid it from Coding Plan users. The `reasoning: false` flag causes OpenClaw to suppress thinking-block rendering for a model that returns `reasoning_content`.
- **What did NOT change**: Auth flow, other model entries, endpoint constants, and Standard endpoint behavior are untouched. Global Coding Plan (`coding-intl.dashscope.aliyuncs.com`) behavior is not claimed.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations (Qwen / DashScope provider catalog)
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Standalone catalog update; no linked issue.
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior addressed**: `qwen3.6-plus` was suppressed on Coding Plan CN and showed `reasoning: false`. After this patch it is available on `coding.dashscope.aliyuncs.com` and the reasoning flag is accurate.
- **Environment tested**: DashScope CN Coding Plan endpoint (`https://coding.dashscope.aliyuncs.com/v1`), live API key, direct HTTP.
- **Steps run after the patch**:
  1. Sent a completion request for `qwen3.6-plus` to `https://coding.dashscope.aliyuncs.com/v1` with a live Coding Plan API key.
  2. Inspected response for `reasoning_content` and `reasoning_tokens` confirming thinking-mode support.
- **Observed result**: `qwen3.6-plus` accepted on Coding Plan CN endpoint; `reasoning_content` and `reasoning_tokens` present in response, confirming `reasoning: true` is correct.
- **Evidence**:

<details>
<summary>Live response — qwen3.6-plus on coding.dashscope.aliyuncs.com</summary>

```text
POST https://coding.dashscope.aliyuncs.com/v1/chat/completions
Authorization: Bearer sk-<redacted>
```

```json
{
  "model": "qwen3.6-plus",
  "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "What is 2+2? Answer in one word."}],
  "max_tokens": 50
}
```

```json
{
  "choices": [{
    "message": {
      "content": "Four",
      "reasoning_content": "Here's a thinking process:\n\n1. Question: \"What is 2+2?\"\n2. 2 + 2 = 4\n3. One word: Four",
      "role": "assistant"
    },
    "finish_reason": "stop",
    "index": 0
  }],
  "usage": {
    "prompt_tokens": 22,
    "completion_tokens": 227,
    "total_tokens": 249,
    "completion_tokens_details": {"reasoning_tokens": 221, "text_tokens": 227}
  },
  "model": "qwen3.6-plus"
}
```

`qwen3.6-plus` accepted on `coding.dashscope.aliyuncs.com`; `reasoning_content` and `reasoning_tokens` confirmed.

</details>

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`
- Risk summary: Catalog metadata change only. Removes an incorrect suppression; no new endpoint, auth flow, or runtime surface introduced.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- Endpoint: DashScope CN Coding Plan (`https://coding.dashscope.aliyuncs.com/v1`)
- Auth: DashScope Coding Plan API key (`sk-<redacted>`)

### Steps

1. POST completion request with `"model": "qwen3.6-plus"` to `https://coding.dashscope.aliyuncs.com/v1`.
2. Inspect response for `reasoning_content` and `completion_tokens_details.reasoning_tokens`.

### Expected

`qwen3.6-plus` responds on Coding Plan CN with `reasoning_content` present.

### Actual

Matches expected.

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios**: `qwen3.6-plus` live on `coding.dashscope.aliyuncs.com`; `reasoning_content` present (flag corrected to `true`).
- **Edge cases checked**: None beyond CN Coding Plan endpoint.
- **What I did NOT verify**: Global Coding Plan endpoint (`coding-intl.dashscope.aliyuncs.com`) — not maintained by this contributor.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes` — removes a suppression; Coding Plan CN users who previously could not select `qwen3.6-plus` can now do so. No existing working configuration is broken.
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk**: `qwen3.6-plus` may not be available on all Coding Plan CN accounts (account-level activation required).
  - Mitigation: Users without access will receive a vendor-side model-not-found error, same as any unavailable model; no crash or data loss.
- **Risk**: Global Coding Plan (`coding-intl.dashscope.aliyuncs.com`) behavior unknown — suppression removed for that endpoint too as a side effect.
  - Mitigation: If Global Coding Plan does not support `qwen3.6-plus`, affected users will see a vendor error. A follow-up can re-add a suppression scoped to `coding-intl` only if confirmed unavailable there.

target.title: fix(whatsapp): Add group admin privacy controls
target.body:
Closes #69233

## What changed

This adds an optional per-group WhatsApp admin override via `channels.whatsapp.groups.<jid>.admin` and wildcard `channels.whatsapp.groups."*".admin`.

When an admin is configured for a group scope:
- the configured E.164 number bypasses mention gating for that group
- non-admin group members cannot run slash commands in that group
- the configured admin remains able to run slash commands for that group

When no admin is configured, the existing group command authorization behavior is preserved.

## Why

The issue described a privacy gap in mixed-trust WhatsApp groups: non-admin members could still probe bot behavior through mentions and slash commands. The first pass of this feature covered the basic config and enforcement, but upstream readiness also needed the behavior pinned down for wildcard group config and for the no-admin-configured fallback path.

## User impact

Users can now designate a WhatsApp group admin by phone number and use that as a privacy boundary for command discovery in group chats.

This is intentionally backwards-compatible:
- groups without `admin` continue using the existing command authorization rules
- exact-group and wildcard group config both work
- docs now describe the new config key

## Root cause

OpenClaw already had group policy, allowlists, and mention gating, but it did not have a per-group owner/admin concept for WhatsApp. That meant there was no narrow way to let a trusted operator manage the bot in a group without exposing command access to other participants covered by broader allowlist rules.

## Validation

- `npm exec pnpm@10.9.0 -- test extensions/whatsapp/src/group-policy.test.ts`
- `npm exec pnpm@10.9.0 -- test extensions/whatsapp/src/inbound/access-control.test.ts`
- `npm exec pnpm@10.9.0 -- test extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor/group-activation.test.ts`
- `npm exec pnpm@10.9.0 -- test src/config/config.schema-regressions.test.ts`
- `npm exec pnpm@10.9.0 -- test extensions/whatsapp/src`
- `npm exec pnpm@10.9.0 -- tsgo:core`
- `npm exec pnpm@10.9.0 -- tsgo:extensions`
- `npm exec pnpm@10.9.0 -- check`
- `npm exec pnpm@10.9.0 -- build`

## AI assistance

- AI-assisted: yes, with Codex
- Testing: fully tested locally with the commands listed above
- Bot review threads addressed and resolved after follow-up fixes

- adds opt-in structured core Doctor checks for `core/doctor/gateway-health` and `core/doctor/gateway-daemon`

## Real behavior proof

Focused regression proof on current head covers a remote Gateway URL containing userinfo and a sensitive query token. The structured finding target is redacted as `wss://***:***@gateway.example.test/rpc?token=***&safe=value`, and the serialized findings do not contain the original userinfo or token value.

Attempted default `node scripts/run-node.mjs doctor --lint --json`, but the local source command timed out in this fresh worktree. The new checks are default-disabled and covered by focused lint-flow tests plus explicit `--only` CLI proof above.

target.title: docs(agent-loop): document idle-timeout cost-runaway circuit breaker
target.body:
## What

Adds a bullet to the Timeouts section of `docs/concepts/agent-loop.md` documenting the idle-timeout circuit breaker introduced in #76345.

## Why

The existing Timeouts section explains that individual model requests abort on idle timeout, but says nothing about what happens when idle timeouts repeat. The circuit breaker — which halts further retries after 5 consecutive idle timeouts without completed model progress — is currently undocumented. Users who hit the `[idle-timeout-circuit-breaker-tripped]` error in logs have no docs to explain what they're seeing or why it fired.

## Accuracy

All claims in the added bullet are sourced directly from `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/idle-timeout-breaker.ts` and the CHANGELOG entry for #76345:
- Cap of 5: `MAX_CONSECUTIVE_IDLE_TIMEOUTS_BEFORE_OUTPUT = 5`
- Reset condition: `completedModelProgress` (completed text or tool-call progress), not partial output tokens
- Scope: fires across auth-profile retries inside the same embedded run
- Incident numbers: from the original issue #76293 report

target.title: diag(cli-runner): attribute AbortError trigger source
target.body:
## Problem

`[agent/cli-backend] claude live session turn failed: ... error=AbortError` log lines have no detail field, so operators cannot tell which of four distinct trigger paths fired:

1. User-typed abort word (`stop`, `wait`, etc. via `ABORT_TRIGGERS`)
2. `abortForRestart` from compaction-restart / drain
3. Mid-turn `/model` switch (`requestLiveSessionModelSwitch`)
4. Explicit `chat.abort` RPC

This makes the bug pattern impossible to fix without speculation. We've been seeing 20+ AbortError lines / 10 days across our deployment with no way to attribute them.

## Change

Diagnostic enrichment only — no behavior change.

- `src/agents/cli-runner/claude-live-session.ts`:
  - `createAbortError(reason?)` now accepts and includes the upstream abort reason in the error message (was: zero-arg, fixed `"CLI run aborted"`).
  - `failTurn` appends `detail="..."` (truncated to 240 chars) to the log line.
  - The two abort paths split: `abortFromSignal` reads `params.context.params.abortSignal.reason` (preserves `controller.abort(reason)` payload — `UserAbortError` for stop-words, `Error("Reply operation aborted for restart")` for compaction, etc.); `abortFromReplyBackend` tags `replyBackend:<reason>` (`user_abort`, `restart`, or `superseded`).
- `src/agents/cli-runner/execute.ts`: same `createCliAbortError(reason?)` upgrade for the non-live CLI path.
- `src/gateway/chat-abort.ts`: `abortChatRunById` calls `controller.abort("chat-abort:<stopReason>")` instead of `.abort()`, so the existing `stopReason` (e.g., `"rpc"`, `"stop"`, `"timeout"`) propagates.

## Effect on log lines

Before:
\`\`\`
... error=AbortError
\`\`\`

After (one of):
\`\`\`
... error=AbortError detail="CLI run aborted: AbortError: Reply operation aborted by user"
... error=AbortError detail="CLI run aborted: AbortError: Reply operation aborted for restart"
... error=AbortError detail="CLI run aborted: replyBackend:restart"
... error=AbortError detail="CLI run aborted: chat-abort:rpc"
\`\`\`

That is sufficient to attribute each abort to its real source (operator stop-word vs. compaction restart vs. model switch vs. RPC) without re-investigating from scratch.

## Tests

- New: `src/agents/cli-runner/abort-reason.test.ts` — 7 unit tests covering string / Error / object / nullish abort-reason inputs for both `createAbortError` (live-session path) and `createCliAbortError` (non-live path). All pass.
- Existing: `src/gateway/chat-abort.test.ts` 4/4 pass; `src/agents/cli-runner/` 35/35 pass.
- `pnpm tsgo -p tsconfig.core.json` and `pnpm tsgo -p tsconfig.core.test.json` both clean.

`pnpm check:changed` not run — would be appreciated as part of CI verification.

## Out of scope

I deliberately did NOT raise the abort grace timeout, change `ABORT_TRIGGERS`, or alter compaction-restart policy. Without trigger evidence those would be guesses. This PR is the prerequisite — once a few new aborts arrive with attributed reasons, the actual fix becomes obvious.

## Provenance

This change was scoped and authored by a Claude Code background agent during a source-level diagnostic of multiple gateway issues. Full investigation writeup (root causes, failing call paths, what wasn't fixed and why) is in `DIAGNOSTIC.md` on the branch but not included in the diff so it doesn't pollute the repo.

target.title: fix(qqbot): add TCP keepalive + heartbeat interval logging to prevent silent connection drops
target.body:
### Problem

**Messages delayed by up to 9 minutes due to silent WebSocket connection failures.**

On 2026-05-04 07:13, a user sent a message via QQBot. The message was not received until 07:22:18 — **9 minutes of delay**. During this entire period, the OpenClaw gateway showed no errors, no reconnection attempts, and no heartbeat failures. The connection appeared healthy but was in fact dead.

**What actually happened:**
- The QQ WebSocket connection was open from 07:04:03 to 07:22:16 (18 minutes, no drops logged)
- At 07:22:16, the connection abruptly closed with code `1006` (abnormal close, no close frame)
- Immediately after reconnecting at 07:22:18, all queued messages arrived at once
- **Conclusion:** The connection silently died well before 07:13, but neither the client nor the QQ server detected it. Messages were buffered server-side and only delivered after the client reconnect.

**Why no detection?** The existing QQBot gateway implementation has two gaps:
1. **No TCP keepalive** — The `ws` library was instantiated without `setKeepAlive`, meaning the OS-level TCP connection is not probed. Network middleboxes (routers, NAT, firewalls) silently drop idle connections without sending RST/FIN.
2. **No heartbeat timeout detection** — If the server sends a `HEARTBEAT_ACK` on time, the client assumes the connection is healthy. But if the `HEARTBEAT_ACK` is lost in transit (because the underlying TCP path is dead), the client never notices. There is no timeout or ACK validation.

**Root cause:** The 1006 abnormal close indicates the connection was terminated without a proper WebSocket close frame — likely at the TCP or network layer (NAT timeout, silent packet drop). The client had no way to detect this before receiving a message, because there was no lower-level keepalive probe and no heartbeat ACK timeout.

### Solution

**Three changes in `extensions/qqbot/src/engine/gateway/gateway-connection.ts`:**

#### 1. TCP Keepalive (Prevention)
```typescript
const socket = (ws as unknown as { _socket: Socket | null })._socket;
if (socket) {
  socket.setKeepAlive(true, 30_000);  // probe every 30s
  socket.setTimeout(60_000);          // 60s with no data → timeout
  socket.on("timeout", () => {
    log?.warn?.(`WebSocket socket timeout — closing and reconnecting`);
    ws.close();  // triggers existing reconnect flow
  });
}
```

- `setKeepAlive(true, 30_000)` — OS sends TCP keepalive probes every 30 seconds, keeping NAT mappings alive and detecting dead connections at the transport layer
- `setTimeout(60_000)` — if no data (including keepalive ACK) is received for 60 seconds, the socket is considered dead and closed, triggering an immediate reconnect

#### 2. Heartbeat Interval Logging (Debugging)
```typescript
const interval = d.heartbeat_interval;
this.ctx.log?.info(`Heartbeat interval: ${interval}ms (${(interval / 1000).toFixed(1)}s)`);
```

- Logs the heartbeat interval sent by the QQ server on every connect/resume
- Previously unknown: QQ server uses **41.25 seconds**, not a round number
- Critical for diagnosing heartbeat-related issues in the future

#### 3. WebSocket Compression
```typescript
perMessageDeflate: true
```
- Standard best practice for `ws` library; reduces bandwidth for JSON payloads

### Verification

- **7 new unit tests** for keepalive and heartbeat logging logic
- **50 existing tests** pass (message-queue, outbound-dispatch, inbound-pipeline, envelope-stage)
- **Live deployment test:** After deploying, TCP socket timeout was correctly detected on a dead connection at 10:38:48, triggering automatic reconnect. Heartbeat interval logged as `41250ms (41.3s)`.

### Expected Impact

- **Before:** Dead connections go undetected for minutes. Users see message delays of 5-10 minutes. Only a manual restart or the next server-side close triggers reconnect.
- **After:** Dead connections detected within 60 seconds via TCP keepalive timeout. Automatic reconnect restores message delivery. Heartbeat interval visible in logs for troubleshooting.

target.body:
## Summary
- add `authProfileOverride` to model-resolve hook results alongside provider/model overrides
- keep explicit user-pinned auth profiles protected from hook rotation
- preserve legacy auto auth-profile markers through queued follow-up runs so they do not become user-pinned
- update docs, changelog, and focused regression coverage

Supersedes #71862 with the same feature ported onto latest `main` and with the ClawSweeper findings fixed.

## Validation
- `pnpm test src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/setup.test.ts src/agents/live-model-switch.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply-run.media-only.test.ts src/plugins/hooks.model-override-wiring.test.ts src/plugins/hooks.phase-hooks.test.ts src/plugins/hooks.before-agent-start.test.ts`

- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md docs/concepts/agent-loop.md docs/gateway/configuration-reference.md docs/plugins/hooks.md docs/tools/plugin.md src/agents/command/attempt-execution.ts src/agents/live-model-switch.test.ts src/agents/live-model-switch.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/params.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/setup.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/setup.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-auth-profile.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.ts src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply-run.media-only.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply-run.ts src/auto-reply/reply/queue/state.ts src/auto-reply/reply/queue/types.ts src/plugins/hook-before-agent-start.types.ts src/plugins/hooks.before-agent-start.test.ts src/plugins/hooks.model-override-wiring.test.ts src/plugins/hooks.phase-hooks.test.ts src/plugins/hooks.ts`
- `pnpm check:test-types`
- `pnpm build`
- `pnpm check:changed`

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Trusted hooks should be able to prefer an auth profile for model routing, while explicit user-pinned profiles still win.
- Real environment tested: My production OpenClaw VPS, Asuman, updated to OpenClaw 2026.5.4 with the same auth-profile override logic applied as a production patch. Gateway, ZeroAPI, NoldoMem, and Slack were all loaded.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Verified the deployed gateway version, readiness endpoint, auth-profile override markers in the built runner, and gateway startup logs after restarting the gateway.
- Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the real VPS checks:

```text
$ openclaw --version
OpenClaw 2026.5.4 (325df3e)

$ curl -fsS http://127.0.0.1:18789/readyz | head -c 160
{"ready":true,"failing":[],"uptimeMs":707101,"eventLoop":{"degraded":false,

$ grep auth-profile hook markers
/usr/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/pi-embedded-X0afS0ip.js:1479: log$1.info(`[hooks] auth profile preferred as ${modelResolveOverride.authProfileOverride}`);
/usr/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/pi-embedded-X0afS0ip.js:1821: const hookPreferredProfileId = params.authProfileIdSource === "user" ? void 0 : hookSelection.authProfileOverride?.trim() || void 0;
/usr/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/pi-embedded-X0afS0ip.js:1822: const preferredProfileId = hookPreferredProfileId ?? (pluginHarnessOwnsTransport ? resolvePluginHarnessPreferredProfileId() : requestedProfileId);
/usr/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/pi-embedded-X0afS0ip.js:1823: const preferredProfileIdSource = hookPreferredProfileId ? "auto" : params.authProfileIdSource;

$ journalctl ZeroAPI startup excerpt
[plugins] ZeroAPI Router v3.8.12 loaded ...
```

- Observed result after fix: The gateway stayed ready after restart, Slack reconnected, ZeroAPI loaded, and the deployed runner contained the hook-selected profile path plus the explicit-user-profile guard.
- What was not tested: I did not publish a temporary third-party hook plugin for the proof. Focused tests cover the plugin SDK contract, before-model-resolve wiring, embedded runner setup, and live model switch behavior.

target.title: fix(cli): exit after hooks inspection output
target.body:
## Summary

- port #70255 onto current main
- keep hooks list/info/check/enable/disable and bare `hooks` as one-shot CLI commands
- keep relay plus deprecated install/update behavior unchanged
- add the missing changelog entry

## Why

Plugin diagnostics can load third-party hook plugins. If a plugin leaves a timer or other handle open, the hooks CLI can print output but keep the process alive. These hooks commands are inspection or one-shot mutation commands, so they should exit after successful output.

Supersedes #70255.

## Tests

target.title: fix(discord): preserve target intent during normalization
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Discord-like explicit DM targets could be normalized through the id-like target path without preserving the intended direct-message kind.
- Why it matters: A Discord target that is syntactically a user/direct target should stay routed as a user/direct target instead of falling back to generic group/channel inference.
- What changed: Shared target-kind inference now lives in `target-normalization`, and both outbound target resolution and cron delivery pass the inferred kind into id-like plugin resolution.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No Discord runtime sending behavior, config schema, plugin manifest, or user-facing docs changed.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes N/A
- Related N/A
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Explicit Discord direct-message targets such as `user:<id>` preserve inferred user/direct intent when id-like target resolution runs for outbound and cron delivery.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on Windows/PowerShell, Discord plugin configured with a real bot token and a redacted explicit `user:<id>` target.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran local OpenClaw Discord outbound/cron delivery paths against an explicit `user:<id>` Discord target using the patched branch, then compared the live delivery result with the new resolver/cron coverage.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Existing live-result report from @AScriver after the local Discord run, with private IDs and tokens redacted:

```text
Scenario: Discord outbound/cron delivery to an explicit user:<id> target
Result: delivered to the intended DM recipient and preserved explicit user target intent instead of falling back to generic group/channel inference
Coverage alignment: matches the targeted resolver and cron tests added in this PR
```

- Observed result after fix: Discord delivered to the intended DM recipient, and the explicit user/direct target intent was preserved instead of falling back to generic group/channel inference.
- What was not tested: Raw Discord token and private IDs are not published; no additional non-Discord channels were tested live.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Source-level before-state showed the id-like resolver did not receive inferred `preferredKind` for outbound/cron paths; the targeted regression tests cover that missing handoff.
## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: id-like target resolution received only the raw target string, so the plugin resolver could lose the caller's inferred direct-message intent after normalization.
- Missing detection / guardrail: tests covered id-like fallback and directory inference separately, but not propagation of inferred DM/user kind into plugin id-like resolution.
- Contributing context (if known): Discord target parsing can distinguish user/direct targets before the generic id-like normalization path runs.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/infra/outbound/target-resolver.test.ts`, `src/cron/isolated-agent/delivery-target.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: inferred direct-message/user kind is passed into id-like plugin target resolution.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it validates the shared resolver seam and cron delivery call path without needing a live Discord bot.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Discord-targeted delivery paths preserve explicit direct-message intent more reliably when resolving id-like targets. No config or command syntax changes.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
Discord DM-like target -> generic id-like resolution -> kind may default away from user/direct intent

After:
Discord DM-like target -> infer user/direct kind -> id-like resolution receives preferredKind=user -> direct target preserved
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Windows, PowerShell
- Runtime/container: local repo checkout, Node/pnpm from workspace
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Discord target routing logic
- Relevant config (redacted): N/A

### Steps

1. Resolve a plugin id-like target whose channel messaging adapter infers `direct` chat type.
2. Resolve a cron delivery target for an explicit DM-like target.
3. Assert the id-like resolver receives `preferredKind: "user"` and preserves the returned target.

### Expected

- The id-like plugin resolver receives the inferred user/direct kind.
- Cron delivery preserves explicit DM intent after target normalization.

### Actual

- Verified by the new focused tests.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Targeted validation:

```bash
pnpm test src/infra/outbound/target-resolver.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/delivery-target.test.ts
pnpm check:changed
```

## Human Verification (required)

What I personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: local Discord outbound/cron delivery to a redacted explicit `user:<id>` target was reported after this patch; target resolver and cron delivery target unit/seam coverage also passed locally.
- Edge cases checked: id-like raw targets continue passing `preferredKind: "group"`; explicit DM-like targets pass `preferredKind: "user"`.
- What I did **not** verify: raw Discord token/private IDs are intentionally not published; no additional non-Discord channels were tested live.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`): No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: shared target kind inference could affect non-Discord channels that expose `inferTargetChatType`.
  - Mitigation: the helper preserves existing fallback behavior, maps `channel` to `group` where directory lookup expects groups, and adds focused coverage for the new id-like propagation path.

target.title: fix(plugins): supplement ClawHub artifact metadata
target.body:
## Summary

- Accept current ClawHub artifact resolver responses where `artifact` is the package artifact summary shape.
- When the resolver response has a legacy zip artifact but no archive verification metadata, load the version metadata and use its `sha256hash` or `files[]` for verification.
- Add a regression test for the current `clawhub:zeroapi@3.8.9` response shape.

## Why

The ClawHub artifact endpoint can return a legacy zip artifact summary while the install verification metadata is only present on the version endpoint. OpenClaw rejected that package as missing verification metadata, even though the package was valid and installable.

## Tests

- `pnpm test src/plugins/clawhub.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:core`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/infra/clawhub.ts src/plugins/clawhub.ts src/plugins/clawhub.test.ts && git diff --check`
- `pnpm check:changed`

## Real-world validation

- `HOME=$tmp/home OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=$tmp/state OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=$tmp/openclaw.json pnpm openclaw plugins install clawhub:zeroapi@3.8.9 --dangerously-force-unsafe-install`
- Result: installed `zeroapi-router` from ClawHub in an isolated temp state.

## AI-assisted

Yes. I used Codex and reviewed the changed code and tests.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Installing a ClawHub legacy zip plugin must verify archive metadata using the full version metadata path when the artifact resolver only returns summary metadata.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS, using this PR branch and an isolated temporary `OPENCLAW_HOME`, installing the live `clawhub:zeroapi` package.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran the real CLI install path from the PR branch: `OPENCLAW_HOME=/tmp/openclaw-real-proof-NSAg8l pnpm openclaw plugins install clawhub:zeroapi --force`.
- Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the real CLI command:

```text
OPENCLAW_HOME:/tmp/openclaw-real-proof-NSAg8l
exit:0
> openclaw@2026.5.4 openclaw /Users/doruk/Desktop/Asuman/.worktrees/openclaw-clawhub-artifact-metadata
> node scripts/run-node.mjs plugins install clawhub:zeroapi --force

Resolving clawhub:zeroapi...
ClawHub code-plugin zeroapi@3.8.12 channel=community verification=source-linked

ClawHub package "zeroapi" is community...

Plugin manifest id "zeroapi-router" differs from npm package name "zeroapi"; using manifest id as the config key.

Restart the gateway to load plugins.
stderr_tail: [plugins] zeroapi-config.json not found. Run /zeroapi to configure.
```

- Observed result after fix: The live ClawHub plugin install resolved version metadata, downloaded and extracted the plugin, and exited with status 0 in a fresh temporary OpenClaw home.
- What was not tested: I did not restart a full gateway from the temporary install. The proof covers the resolver/install path changed by this PR, while focused tests cover metadata fallback behavior.

target.title: fix: prune all history images when current turn has fresh attachments
target.body:
## Problem

When sending a new image for analysis, stale image data from previous conversation turns can contaminate the model's interpretation of the current image. Reported in #66702.

## Solution

- Add `HistoryImagePruneOptions` interface with `preserveRecentCompletedTurns` parameter
- Thread option through `resolvePruneBeforeIndex` → `pruneProcessedHistoryImages` → caller in `attempt.ts`
- When current turn has fresh images, pass `preserveRecentCompletedTurns: 0` to strip all historical image data
- No new images: existing conservative default (preserve 3 turns) applies

Backward-compatible. Existing tests unchanged.

Fixes #66702

target.title: feat(telegram): add acknowledgement sticker support
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Telegram does not support streaming text, so users get no immediate interactive feedback while OpenClaw generates a response.
- Why it matters: Ack reactions help, but stickers are more visible and expressive for Telegram conversations.
- What changed: Added configurable Telegram ack stickers with scope control, optional silent delivery, optional removal after reply, config schema/docs, SDK feedback support, and Telegram tests.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): Existing ack reaction behavior remains supported; this does not add text streaming to Telegram or change default Telegram access policy.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature

- [x] API / contracts

- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: N/A
- Missing detection / guardrail: N/A
- Contributing context (if known): Telegram lacks streaming text support, so acknowledgement feedback is handled through channel feedback primitives.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

N/A

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `src/agents/identity.test.ts`
  - `extensions/telegram/src/send.test.ts`
  - `extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-context.ack-sticker.test.ts`
  - `extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: Telegram resolves ackSticker config, sends the configured sticker before dispatching the agent turn, and optionally removes it after reply.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: It covers config resolution, channel feedback shape, Telegram send behavior, and dispatch cleanup without requiring live Telegram credentials.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Existing ack reaction tests covered reaction behavior only.
- If no new test is added, why not: New tests are added.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Telegram users can configure `ackSticker` globally, per account, per direct/group chat, or per topic. When enabled, OpenClaw sends a Telegram sticker acknowledgement before generating the response. Existing ack reactions continue to work.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
Telegram message -> OpenClaw agent loop -> Telegram final reply

After:
Telegram message -> ack sticker -> OpenClaw agent loop -> Telegram final reply -> optional ack sticker cleanup
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: Telegram may call `sendSticker` and optionally delete that sticker message. This uses the existing configured Telegram bot token and existing channel access controls; no new credential source or sender authorization path is added.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22, pnpm
- Model/provider: Not required for unit coverage
- Integration/channel (if any): Telegram
- Relevant config (redacted):

```json5
{
  channels: {
    telegram: {
      enabled: true,
      ackSticker: {
        fileId: "CAAC...",
        scope: "direct",
        silent: true,
        removeAfterReply: true,
      },
    },
  },
}
```

### Steps

1. Configure Telegram `ackSticker.fileId` with a sendable Telegram sticker file id.
2. Start the OpenClaw gateway with Telegram enabled.
3. Send a DM to the Telegram bot.

### Expected

- Telegram sends the configured acknowledgement sticker before the agent response.
- The agent response is delivered normally.
- If `removeAfterReply` is enabled, the acknowledgement sticker is deleted after the final reply.

### Actual

- Unit and config tests pass for the implemented behavior.
- Live Telegram verification was attempted locally but blocked by local gateway token/env setup issues, not by the ackSticker implementation.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after

## Human Verification (required)

target.title: fix(memory, builtin backend): eagerly preload session transcript listeners at gateway startup
target.body:
## Summary (scope narrowed to builtin backend per Codex review)

Eagerly preload `MemoryIndexManager` at gateway startup for every agent whose `agents.defaults.memorySearch.sources` (or per-agent overlay) includes `"sessions"`, **when using the builtin session-file backend**. This arms `ensureSessionListener()` before the first `sessionTranscriptUpdate` event fires, so `/reset` and `/new` archive emits actually land in the listener set and get indexed into `chunks` without the user having to run `openclaw memory index --force`.

## Why it was broken

`MemoryIndexManager` is lazy-loaded on the first `memory_search` tool call. If a user types `/reset` or `/new` before that first search, the builtin `archiveFileOnDisk` emit lands in an empty `SESSION_TRANSCRIPT_LISTENERS` set and is silently dropped, so the resulting `.jsonl.reset.<iso>` / `.jsonl.deleted.<iso>` archives never enter `chunks`. Only `memory index --force` recovers them (which is #76311 / #76452's escape hatch but is not a cold-start fix).

## Why builtin-only (Codex review response)

Thanks @clawsweeper for the P2 catch on the qmd path. The initial revision of this PR treated any successful `getActiveMemorySearchManager` result as listener-armed, but under the qmd backend that call returns the `FallbackMemoryManager` wrapper whose inner `MemoryIndexManager` is only constructed by `fallbackFactory`. So on qmd, merely obtaining the wrapper does NOT run `ensureSessionListener()`, and the cold-start race still drops `sessionTranscriptUpdate` emits.

Three options were on the table (per Codex):
1. Explicitly preload the builtin `MemoryIndexManager` listener owner even for qmd — requires reaching into the FallbackMemoryManager fallback slot.
2. Add qmd-native transcript event handling inside `qmd-manager.ts`.
3. **Narrow this PR to builtin-only and defer qmd to a follow-up.**

This revision takes **option 3**: added an explicit early-return guard `if (params.cfg.memory?.backend === "qmd") { ... return; }` at the top of `startGatewayMemorySessionListeners`, with a log line making the scope decision visible, plus a regression test (`skips preload entirely when memory.backend is qmd (Codex review scope guard)`). The changelog entry is also retitled `Memory/sessions (builtin backend):` and calls out the intentional qmd deferral.

## Independent verification (builtin only)

CarHer builtin-backend deployment (`carher-199` baseline). Three cold restarts, same container, identical `/reset` sequence:

| | pre-preload | post-preload |
|---|---|---|
| archive emit lands in listener set | ❌ dropped | ✅ captured |
| `chunks` delta after 30s | +0 | +41 |
| verified commit | `main @ 9a49d143c819` | `HEAD of this PR` |

## What is NOT included

- qmd backend session-listener arming (follow-up PR, tracked in body + changelog).
- Changes to `qmd-manager.ts` transcript event handling.
- Changes to `FallbackMemoryManager` fallback construction.

Refs #56131. AI-assisted.

## Summary

Fixes #76638 — when a diary entry had long content (10+ paragraphs), the day-chip navigation bar at the top would be pushed out of the viewport, making it impossible to switch between dates.

Changed `.dreams-diary__daychips`:

- Bumped `z-index` from 1 to 2 to stay above diary entries

target.title: docs(prometheus): warn that plugins.allow is strict-mode
target.body:
## Summary

Adds a `<Warning>` callout to the Prometheus Quick Start clarifying that `plugins.allow` is **strict-mode** — when set, only listed plugins are eligible to load. The current Quick Start example shows `allow: ["diagnostics-prometheus"]` without warning that this disables every other configured plugin on a real running gateway.

## AI assistance disclosure

This PR was drafted with **Claude Code** (Anthropic's CLI agent).

- [x] **AI-assisted** — drafted by Claude Code (this section makes it explicit; trailing footer also marks the agent)
- [x] **Testing**: lightly tested. Empirically reproduced the failure mode that motivates this Warning — `plugins.allow: ["diagnostics-prometheus"]` dropped a 72-plugin running v2026.5.2 gateway to 9 enabled plugins; recovered via `openclaw config unset plugins.allow` + gateway restart. Did NOT build the docs site locally — render check left for CI / maintainer.
- [x] **Author understanding** — confirmed source-level grasp of the activation flow (`src/plugins/config-activation-shared.ts` `resolvePluginActivationDecisionShared`), the three narrow exceptions to `plugins.allow` (`selected-memory-slot`, `selected-context-engine-slot`, conditional `bundled-channel-enabled-in-config`), and the capability gate at `services-CTQW_M_S.js:15` that motivated cross-referenced #76628.
- [ ] **`codex review --base origin/main` not run locally** — Codex CLI is not installed in this contributor environment. Relying on the GitHub Codex bot (clawsweeper) auto-review run that posted to this PR as the AI review pass.
- [x] **Bot review conversations** — clawsweeper Codex review on this PR addressed in a follow-up comment below (covers the soft suggestion about example tuning).

## Why

Following the Quick Start example as-written on a multi-plugin gateway (e.g. one running `memory-core`, `active-memory`, `memory-wiki`, `telegram`, etc.) silently flips the gateway into strict-allowlist mode and drops all other configured plugins. On a 71-plugin running gateway (mid-sized self-hosted setup), the example dropped the enabled-plugin count from 72 to **9** — including critical plugins like `memory-core`, `active-memory`, and the configured Telegram channel — until rolled back via `openclaw config unset plugins.allow`.

The warning surface for this is misleading in two ways:

1. The startup log line `[plugins] plugins.allow is empty; discovered non-bundled plugins may auto-load: <id>. Set plugins.allow to explicit trusted ids.` *implies* setting `plugins.allow` is the right next step. It isn't, in the general case.
2. The schema description for `plugins.allow` is correct (*"when set, only listed plugins are eligible to load"*), but the Quick Start snippet doesn't reproduce that semantics in user-visible documentation.

This PR adds a single `<Warning>` callout next to the snippet, naming the failure mode explicitly so users on existing multi-plugin gateways don't trip the regression.

## Cross-references

- **#75575** (open) — *Honor `plugins.allow` on runtime provider paths (currently force-loaded) — propose `plugins.bundledMode` opt-in*. Same family of `plugins.allow` semantic confusion. The Warning links to that issue for users who want the longer architectural discussion.
- **#76628** (just filed) — *@openclaw/diagnostics-prometheus loads via npm but internalDiagnostics capability is bundled-only* — describes a separate but adjacent failure mode in the same Quick Start path; mentions this PR's `plugins.allow` finding as one of the empirical investigations along the way.

## Scope

- Single-file doc change: `docs/gateway/prometheus.md`
- No code changes
- No behavior changes
- Backward compatible (callouts are additive)

## Test plan

- [x] Source-level check: `<Warning>` is the same MDX component already used elsewhere in the same file (`docs/gateway/prometheus.md:19-21`), so no new component dependencies. Indentation matches the surrounding `<Step>` / `<Tabs>` block.
- [ ] Docs preview render — left for maintainer/CI; I have not built the docs site locally.
- [x] Empirically reproduced the regression that this Warning describes (multi-plugin gateway dropping from 72 → 9 enabled plugins after applying the existing snippet, fully recovered by `openclaw config unset plugins.allow` + restart). See **#76628** for the full reproduction.

target.title: fix(zalouser): split messages at line boundaries instead of mid-word
target.body:
## TL;DR

Long bot responses were split mid-word because the default chunk mode cut at exactly 2000 characters with no regard for word or sentence boundaries.

---

## Problem

`DEFAULT_TEXT_CHUNK_MODE = "length"` cuts the outgoing message at exactly the 2000-character limit regardless of whether that lands in the middle of a word. In practice this was observed as a Vietnamese word like "lên" arriving as two separate messages — "lê" followed by "n" — which is unreadable.

The `"newline"` chunk mode already exists in the codebase and correctly breaks at paragraph boundaries → single newlines → whitespace → hard cut (as a last resort). It was never set as the default, so callers that did not explicitly pass `textChunkMode: "newline"` got the hard-cut behavior.

---

## Invariant

> When a message is split into chunks, each chunk boundary must fall at a natural break (paragraph, newline, or whitespace) unless no such break exists within the limit window.

---

## What this PR does

- Changes `DEFAULT_TEXT_CHUNK_MODE` from `"length"` to `"newline"` in `send.ts`
- Adds one regression test: a message with a paragraph break inside the 2000-char window is split at the break, not at position 2000

---

## Why this matters

- **User impact**: Words arrived split across two Zalo messages, making bot responses unreadable in long replies.
- **System impact**: The hard-cut behavior never had a use case — `"newline"` falls back to a hard cut when no natural break exists, so it is strictly better for all inputs.

---

## Safety

- The `splitTextRangesByPreferredBreaks` function (used by `"newline"` mode) falls back to a hard cut when no natural break point is found, so behavior for texts with no whitespace is unchanged.
- All existing tests pass unchanged: they use continuous character strings with no natural break points, which fall through to the same hard-cut behavior as before.
- No change to any other logic.

---

## Test plan

```
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/zalouser/src/send.test.ts
```

- [x] All existing tests pass (13)
- [x] New test: paragraph break within window → split at break, not at position 2000

target.title: fix(zalouser): surface send errors and recover from oversized style payloads
target.body:
## TL;DR

Zalo delivery failures were completely invisible: the monitor never checked send results, error codes were lost, and messages silently disappeared when styled payloads exceeded Zalo's size limit.

---

## Problem

Three compounding gaps caused messages to vanish without any log trace:

1. `sendZaloTextMessage` caught errors but discarded the numeric `error_code` from Zalo's response — callers could not distinguish a transient network error from a Zalo-specific rejection.
2. The `sendText` and `sendMedia` callbacks in the monitor called `sendMessageZalouser` without checking the returned result, so `ok=false` responses were silently dropped.
3. Zalo rejects messages with `error_code=112` when the combined params payload (message text + `textProperties` JSON for styling) exceeds its server-side size limit. Empirically: a ~1420-char Vietnamese message combined with 56+ style ranges (~1871 chars of JSON) triggers this. The message was never delivered and no error appeared in logs.

---

## Invariant

> When Zalo rejects a styled message with error code 112, the message text must still be delivered (without styles), and the failure must be logged if delivery ultimately fails.

---

## What this PR does

- Adds `errorCode?: number` to `ZaloSendResult` so callers can branch on specific Zalo error codes
- Enhances `toErrorMessage` to include the numeric code in the string so it appears in logs
- Extracts the numeric error code from caught errors in `sendZaloTextMessage` and attaches it to the result
- Adds a retry-without-styles path in `sendMessageZalouser` on error code 112: if the styled send fails with 112, the message is re-sent as plain text
- Makes `sendText` and `sendMedia` in the monitor check `result.ok` and emit a structured error log when delivery fails

---

## Why this matters

- **User impact**: Long bot responses with markdown formatting silently failed to reach Zalo users. The bot appeared to respond on the web UI but nothing arrived in the chat.
- **System impact**: Failures were completely unobservable — no logs, no error propagation — making debugging impossible without adding instrumentation manually.

---

## Safety

- No change to message delivery path when everything succeeds
- The retry only triggers on the specific error code 112 with styled content; all other errors are forwarded unchanged
- Existing tests unchanged; 3 new regression tests added

---

## Test plan

```
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/zalouser/src/send.test.ts
```

- [x] All existing tests pass (13)
- [x] New test: retries without styles on error code 112
- [x] New test: propagates error when retry also fails
- [x] New test: does not retry when there are no styles

target.title: fix(sandbox): bump config hash when buildSandboxCreateArgs flags change
target.body:
## Summary

Adds a `SANDBOX_DOCKER_CREATE_ARGS_EPOCH` constant and a `createArgsEpoch` field to both `SandboxHashInput` and `SandboxBrowserHashInput`, so the existing config-hash recreate path also fires when `buildSandboxCreateArgs` adds, removes, or changes container creation flags.

**This PR adds the mechanism only.** It does not ship any flag change itself. Whichever PR adds the first real flag change (for example #74083 adding `--init`) bumps the epoch from the bootstrap value in the same release as the flag.

## Why

`buildSandboxCreateArgs` already drives container recreation via the labelled `openclaw.configHash`, but the hash inputs only capture mount layout (`mountFormatVersion`) and browser security flags (`securityEpoch`). Other docker create flags are invisible to the hash. So adding any new flag in a future PR (`--init`, cgroup limits, sysctls, capabilities, namespace flags, etc.) does NOT invalidate the hash, and existing containers keep running with their old flags forever.

The most-affected pattern is exactly the one #68691 reports: long-running sandbox containers (`scope: "agent"` + `prune.maxAgeDays: 0`) stay alive for weeks and never recreate naturally. Without an epoch in the hash, they never pick up new flags.

## What this PR adds

1. **`SANDBOX_DOCKER_CREATE_ARGS_EPOCH`** in `src/agents/sandbox/constants.ts`, set to a neutral bootstrap value (`"2026-05-bootstrap"`) and date-tagged so reviewers can tell at a glance which release introduced the bump.
2. **Header comment on the constant** explicitly telling future contributors to bump the epoch in the same PR (or a follow-up PR) as the actual flag change, never speculatively. Pre-bumping under a name like `"init-tini"` would let containers be recreated with a hash claiming the new behaviour while the actual create args still lack it.
3. **`createArgsEpoch: string`** in both `SandboxHashInput` and `SandboxBrowserHashInput`. Mirrors the existing `securityEpoch` / `mountFormatVersion` pattern.
4. **Both call sites updated** (`docker.ts:ensureSandboxContainer`, `browser.ts:ensureSandboxBrowser`) to pass the new constant.
5. **`openclaw.createArgsEpoch=...` container label** stamped at create time, alongside the existing `openclaw.mountFormatVersion` label, for diagnostics.
6. **Tests** (`config-hash.test.ts`, `docker.config-hash-recreate.test.ts`) updated; new test "changes when docker create args epoch changes" mirrors the existing security-epoch test using neutral `"epoch-v1"`/`"epoch-v2"` sentinels.
7. **CHANGELOG** entry under `## Unreleased > ### Fixes` describing the mechanism, with `Thanks @aaajiao.` per repo convention.

## Reusability

When future PRs add or change container flags, the contributor only needs to bump the `SANDBOX_DOCKER_CREATE_ARGS_EPOCH` string in the same change and add a one-line history entry to the comment. No new types, no new test scaffolding, no risk of forgetting to invalidate the hash.

## Relationship to #74083 (and #68691)

Originally I had named this epoch after the `--init` rollout. Per clawsweeper P1 review, that was wrong — it would let this PR claim a feature ship that lives in a separate PR. The current value is neutral; the `--init` rollout in #74083 is responsible for bumping the epoch when it lands. With both merged in the same release, the long-running-container pattern reported in #68691 is fully addressed.

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm test src/agents/sandbox/config-hash.test.ts` — existing + new tests pass locally
- [x] `pnpm test src/agents/sandbox/docker.config-hash-recreate.test.ts` — recreate path still asserts hash mismatch triggers `docker rm -f`
- [ ] Maintainer-side: full CI pass (note: the unrelated `checks-node-agentic-cli` failures in `plugins-location-bridges.test.ts` reproduce on upstream/main — see commit `5ecd01ff9` — and have no overlap with sandbox)
- [ ] Live verification: a future flag change (such as #74083) bumps the epoch and a long-running `scope: "agent"` sandbox is recreated on next agent run

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: Fix/cron duplicate name validation
target.body:
Fixes #76160

\`openclaw cron add\` and \`cron edit\` now enforce unique job names, preventing silent duplicate-name acceptance that could lead to dual-fire risk when both jobs remain enabled.

## Problem

Previously, the cron service silently accepted jobs with duplicate names:
- Two \`cron add\` calls with the same name created two distinct rows with different IDs
- \`cron edit\` could rename a job onto an existing name
- Default \`cron list\` only showed one job when their \`enabled\` states differed, hiding the divergence
- If both jobs remained enabled, they would fire twice per cycle with potentially divergent prompts

## Solution

Added name uniqueness validation in the service layer:
- **\`cron.add\`**: Checks for existing job with the same name before creating
- **\`cron.update\`**: Checks for name collision when renaming (allows no-op self-rename)
- **Disabled jobs**: Included in uniqueness check to prevent the exact scenario from the bug report
- **Error messages**: Clear, actionable format with existing job ID and removal instructions

## Implementation

- \`src/cron/service/ops.ts\`: Added \`findJobByName()\` checks in \`add()\` and \`update()\`
- \`src/cron/service/jobs.ts\`: Added \`findJobByName()\` helper function
- \`src/cron/service.duplicate-names.test.ts\`: Comprehensive test suite (13 test cases)

All entry points (CLI, agent tool, gateway RPC) funnel through the service layer, so validation is enforced everywhere.

## Testing

Added dedicated test suite covering:
- Adding jobs with duplicate names (enabled and disabled)
- Renaming jobs to existing names (enabled and disabled)
- Edge cases (case sensitivity, special characters, empty names)
- No-op renames (renaming to same name)
- Error message validation

## Breaking Changes

None. This is a bug fix that prevents invalid state. Operators who were accidentally creating duplicate names will now receive clear error messages with instructions to resolve the conflict.

## Related

- Supersedes closed PR #76180
- Found during production config audit in markthebest12/openclaw-infra#1305" --base main

target.title: refactor: flip sessions and transcripts to sqlite storage
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Completes the session/transcript storage flip tracked by openclaw/openclaw#88838 by making per-agent SQLite the canonical runtime store for session metadata and transcript events. Legacy `sessions.json` and active transcript JSONL files are now doctor import inputs or archive artifacts, not runtime fallback state.

## Why This Change Was Made

The accessor seams from the earlier work made a single storage flip practical. Shipping a mixed file/SQLite runtime would let session metadata and transcript rows drift, so this PR moves import, runtime access, retention, SDK cleanup, and ratchets together behind the permanent accessor boundary.

## User Impact

Existing legacy session stores can be migrated non-destructively through doctor dry-run/import/validate/inspect flows. Runtime session creation, resume, transcript append, compaction, and maintenance use SQLite rows; retention still exports archived JSONL artifacts before deleting rows so support and archive workflows keep inspectable artifacts.

## Evidence

- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.test.src.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/test-src.tsbuildinfo`
- `node scripts/check-session-accessor-boundary.mjs`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/config/sessions/session-accessor.test.ts src/config/sessions/targets.test.ts src/commands/doctor-session-sqlite.test.ts test/scripts/check-session-accessor-boundary.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/session-store-runtime.test.ts src/config/sessions/session-accessor.conformance.test.ts src/config/sessions/store.session-lifecycle-mutation.test.ts src/plugins/contracts/host-hooks.contract.test.ts src/plugins/contracts/session-entry-projection.contract.test.ts src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-runtime-api-guardrails.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/config/sessions/session-accessor.test.ts src/config/sessions/targets.ts src/config/sessions/targets.test.ts src/commands/doctor-session-sqlite.test.ts`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local` clean after two accepted findings were fixed.
- Copied local OpenClaw state proof under `/tmp/openclaw-sqlite-flip-final-20260625T013042Z`: doctor dry-run/import/validate/inspect on `codex-proof` and `codex-image-proof` reported zero issues; import wrote 2 entries plus 15 and 18 transcript events respectively.
- Isolated runtime smoke created an agent SQLite DB, created/resumed a session, appended transcript rows, compacted 4 rows to 3, and archive-before-delete cleanup removed 1 lifecycle entry after writing 1 archive artifact.

target.title: fix(memory-wiki): resolve bridge zero-artifact report in CLI snapshot mode
target.body:
## Summary

Fix `openclaw wiki bridge import` and `openclaw wiki status` returning zero exported memory artifacts while `memory-core` can directly list hundreds of public artifacts from the same config.

**Root Cause**: In CLI snapshot mode, the plugin loader restores the previous memory plugin state after registration, clearing the memory capability from the global plugin state. `listActiveMemoryPublicArtifacts()` depends on `memoryPluginState.capability` being set — when it's undefined, the function returns an empty array.

**Fix**: Add `resolveBridgePublicArtifacts()` which first tries the capability-based path, and falls back to directly calling `listMemoryHostPublicArtifacts` from the public SDK (`openclaw/plugin-sdk/memory-host-core`) when the capability is unavailable. This fallback only activates when the configured memory slot is `"memory-core"`.

**Security boundary**: The fallback uses the **public SDK function** `listMemoryHostPublicArtifacts` rather than importing from memory-core's private `src/`, keeping the implementation on the public plugin SDK seam.

Fixes #85655

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: Bridge artifact listing returns 0 when memory capability is not persisted in CLI snapshot mode.

**Real setup tested**:
- Runtime: Node.js (OpenClaw test suite)
- Test suites: bridge (11), status (7), gateway (15), CLI (15)

**Exact steps or command run after fix**:

```bash
pnpm test -- extensions/memory-wiki/src/bridge.test.ts
pnpm test -- extensions/memory-wiki/src/status.test.ts
pnpm test -- extensions/memory-wiki/src/gateway.test.ts
pnpm test -- extensions/memory-wiki/src/cli.test.ts
```

**After-fix evidence**:

```
✓ bridge.test.ts: 11 tests passed
✓ status.test.ts: 7 tests passed
✓ gateway.test.ts: 15 tests passed
✓ cli.test.ts: 15 tests passed
```

**Observed result after the fix**: All 49 tests pass across 4 related test suites. No regressions.

**What was not tested**: Live Gateway integration test with a real memory-core vault. The source-level reproduction is confirmed by ClawSweeper review.

## Tests and validation

| Test Type | Result | Details |
|-----------|--------|---------|
| Bridge Tests | ✅ | 11/11 passed |
| Status Tests | ✅ | 7/7 passed |
| Gateway Tests | ✅ | 15/15 passed |
| CLI Tests | ✅ | 15/15 passed |
| Index Test | ✅ | 1/1 passed |

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`No` — behavior only changes when capability was returning empty artifacts. Normal Gateway path is unaffected.)

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`No`)

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`No`)

What is the highest-risk area?
- None. The fallback uses the public SDK function `listMemoryHostPublicArtifacts` on the same seam as the capability path.

How is that risk mitigated?
- Only activated when `plugins.slots.memory === "memory-core"` and the capability path returns empty.
- Uses the public SDK `openclaw/plugin-sdk/memory-host-core` — no cross-extension import.
- Falls back to `[]` on any error or when memory slot is not `memory-core`.

## Current review state

What is the next action?
- ClawSweeper re-review after addressing P1 findings.

target.title: [codex] Accept audio and video model catalog inputs
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes #97048.

Generated plugin catalog shards can include `audio` and `video` input modalities for models such as MiniMax-M3 and phi-4 multimodal. The session model registry schema only accepted `text` and `image`, so those generated shards could be rejected and the provider could fall back to an older or incomplete catalog.

## Why This Change Was Made

The canonical model input type and the session registry/config validation paths now accept `audio` and `video` alongside `text` and `image`. The model-list rendering path also preserves those modalities so configured and generated model metadata stay consistent.

## User Impact

Generated catalogs with MiniMax-M3 or similar multimodal models no longer get dropped only because they advertise audio/video inputs. Real MiniMax M3 API verification is still left to the maintainer/user-provided API key.

## Evidence

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/sessions/model-registry.test.ts src/config/zod-schema.models.test.ts src/commands/models/list.rows.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core.tsbuildinfo`

Known local proof note: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugin-sdk/provider-catalog-shared.test.ts` was also tried, but unrelated native streaming usage capability assertions failed there and were not used as proof for this PR.

target.title: fix: strip volatile output from exec result hash to fix no-progress detection (#93917)
target.body:
## Summary

The `genericRepeat` detector in `tool-loop-detection.ts` has two detection paths:

1. **Warning path**: uses `recentCount` — counts all calls with identical args, regardless of result. ✅ Works correctly.
2. **Critical path**: uses `noProgressStreak` — requires **identical result hash** across consecutive calls. ❌ Fails for exec commands.
3. **Global circuit breaker**: also uses `noProgressStreak`. ❌ Same failure mode.

**Root cause**: `hashExecToolOutcome()` includes the full `output` text in the hash for `completed`/`failed` exec calls. When a command fails repeatedly (e.g., SSH timeout, docker connection refused), the error output often varies slightly — different timestamps, different error messages, different connection states — causing the result hash to differ each time. This means `noProgressStreak` is **always 1**, never reaching the critical threshold (default 20) or circuit breaker (default 30), even after **hundreds** of identical failed calls.

Fixes #93917

## Changes

### `src/agents/tool-loop-detection.ts`

- Removed unused `nonEmptyStringField` import (was only used for output field now excluded from hash)
- Removed `text` parameter from `hashExecToolOutcome()` — no longer needed after excluding output from hash
- `completed`/`failed` exec hash now only includes `{status, exitCode, timedOut}` — volatile output text excluded

### `src/agents/tool-loop-detection.test.ts`

Updated two test cases to match the corrected behavior.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

**Behavior addressed**: Repeated exec calls with varying output text but stable `{status, exitCode, timedOut}` now correctly trigger the no-progress circuit breaker.

**Real setup tested**:
- **Runtime**: Node.js v24, TypeScript 5.x
- **Project**: openclaw monorepo, src/agents

**Exact steps or command run after fix**:

```bash
# TypeScript compilation verification
npx tsc --noEmit --pretty src/agents/tool-loop-detection.ts

# Full test suite for tool-loop-detection
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/tool-loop-detection.test.ts --reporter=verbose
```

**After-fix evidence**:

```
$ npx tsc --noEmit --pretty src/agents/tool-loop-detection.ts
TypeScript: No errors found
```

```
 ✓ |agents| tool-loop-detection.test.ts > detectToolCallLoop > blocks repeated completed exec calls despite volatile runtime details
 ✓ |agents| tool-loop-detection.test.ts > detectToolCallLoop > blocks repeated running exec calls despite volatile session details and text
 ✓ |agents| tool-loop-detection.test.ts > detectToolCallLoop > escalates repeated exec calls to the global breaker when status and exitCode are stable
 ✓ |agents| tool-loop-detection.test.ts > detectToolCallLoop > escalates repeated failed exec calls to the global breaker when status and exitCode are stable
 ...
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  59 passed (59)
```

**Observed result after the fix**: Repeated exec calls with varying output but stable `{status, exitCode, timedOut}` now correctly trigger the circuit breaker. All 59 tests pass including 2 updated exec-specific tests. TypeScript compilation clean.

**What was not tested**: Live gateway integration — logic change limited to hash computation; coverage for other tool types unchanged.

## Tests and validation

All 59 tests pass in `src/agents/tool-loop-detection.test.ts`. Two test cases renamed/updated to match corrected behavior.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`No`)
- Internal loop detection change. Stuck exec loops are now correctly detected and blocked.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`No`)

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`No`)

What is the highest-risk area?
- False positives: legitimate repeated `exec` calls with genuinely different output but same exit code could be blocked.

How is that risk mitigated?
- 30 identical calls is already a very high bar. Warning path (10 calls) gives earlier signal. Users can tune thresholds in config.

## Current review state

What is the next action?
- Maintainer review

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?
- CI checks (lint fixed, proof updated)

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?
- N/A (new PR)

target.title: Force manual compaction past empty kept-tail cuts
target.body:
## Problem

Manual `/compact` failed to create a checkpoint when a session reported high context usage but held only a tiny transcript.

Context size is measured from provider usage (`usage.totalTokens`, which includes the cached system prompt and tool definitions), so it can read ~170k even when the actual conversation is just a couple of short messages. In that state the cut-point planner keeps the whole branch within the `keepRecentTokens` (20k) budget and selects **zero** older messages, so `prepareCompaction` returns `undefined`:

- **Automatic** compaction correctly skips — there is no old prefix worth summarizing yet.
- **Manual** `/compact` instead surfaced a confusing `Nothing to compact (session too small)`, even though the user explicitly asked for a checkpoint.

"0 messages to summarize" here means "the retained-tail heuristic found no old prefix to replace" — **not** "the session is empty".

## Fix

1. Add a `force` option to `prepareCompaction`. Manual session/harness compaction pass `force: true`; automatic does not. When the heuristic would summarize zero messages, the forced path summarizes the whole branch so `/compact` produces a real checkpoint.
2. The forced boundary anchors `firstKeptEntryId` on the last **valid cut point** (`findValidCutPoints`, which excludes tool results), not the raw final entry.

## Further context

- **Automatic behavior is unchanged.** Without `force`, the zero-message case still returns `undefined` → skip, so automatic compaction never writes empty no-op summaries.
- **The `force` path is narrowly scoped.** It only changes the zero-old-prefix case; every normal cut runs untouched. Truly empty / already-compacted sessions are still refused — the path proceeds only when there is at least one real message.
- **No content is lost.** The whole branch is summarized, so when manual compaction later collapses the boundary to summary-only (`hardenManualCompactionBoundary`), nothing is dropped silently.
- **The retained tail is always provider-valid.** Anchoring on a valid cut point — the same rule the normal path already trusts — keeps any trailing tool result paired with the assistant tool call that owns it, instead of replaying an orphaned tool result that providers reject. If no valid anchor exists, the path returns `undefined` rather than emitting an orphan.

## Tests
- `pnpm test packages/agent-core/src/harness/compaction/compaction.test.ts` — automatic skip, manual force, and the valid-anchor / no-orphan-tool-result regression
- `pnpm test src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/manual-compaction-boundary.test.ts`
- `pnpm test src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/compact.hooks.test.ts`

target.title: Explain private-network exec failures
target.body:
### Summary

- Adds a targeted exec diagnostic for private/internal network commands that fail with network-looking errors.
- Keeps existing exec/security behavior unchanged; this does not silently allow new private-LAN access.
- Covers the observed `curl` and hostname SSH/private-IP-in-stderr failure shapes from #94032.
- Avoids false positives for successful zero-exit commands whose output merely contains private-network-looking failure text.

### Why

When OpenClaw/Codex `exec` cannot reach a private-LAN target but the same user context can reach it from GUI Terminal, Browser, or a user service, the current output looks like a normal LAN outage. That sends operators toward switch/VPN/L2 debugging even when the failure may be an exec runtime, sandbox, or process-scoped policy mismatch.

This patch annotates that specific failed-command shape with a diagnostic that tells the operator to compare against a same-user shell and inspect exec host/sandbox/runtime policy and local VPN/Network Extension filters.

### Tests

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/bash-tools.exec-runtime.test.ts`

### Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** Private/internal network-looking exec failures now include an explicit OpenClaw exec private-network diagnostic, while successful zero-exit output is left alone.
- **Real environment tested:** Mac mini OpenClaw/Codex shell context, PR-head worktree `/private/tmp/openclaw-pr94045-followup-20260621`, commit `184eb75babd0`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran `runExecProcess` from PR head for these commands:

```sh
curl --connect-timeout 2 -I http://192.168.147.254:8686/
ssh -F /private/tmp/openclaw-pr94045-proof/ssh_config lan-proof-host hostname
printf "saved log line: ssh: connect to host 192.168.147.254 port 22: No route to host\n"
```

- **Evidence after fix:** Current-head exec runtime output:

```text
--- curl-private-ip ---
status=completed exitCode=7
curl: (7) Failed to connect to 192.168.147.254 port 8686 after 0 ms: Couldn't connect to server

(Command exited with code 7)

OpenClaw exec private-network diagnostic: this command targets a private/internal address and failed with a network-looking error. ... (exit code 7)

--- hostname-ssh-private-ip-stderr ---
status=completed exitCode=255
ssh: connect to host 192.168.147.254 port 22: Host is down

(Command exited with code 255)

OpenClaw exec private-network diagnostic: this command targets a private/internal address and failed with a network-looking error. ... (exit code 255)

--- zero-exit-private-looking-log ---
status=completed exitCode=0
saved log line: ssh: connect to host 192.168.147.254 port 22: No route to host
```

- **Observed result after fix:** Both failing private-network cases are annotated. The zero-exit command stays unannotated, which addresses the ClawSweeper false-positive finding.
- **What was not tested:** I did not deploy this branch into the production gateway or attempt to make private-LAN access succeed from exec. That is intentionally out of scope for this diagnostic-only patch.

Fixes #94032
